title: King Lear
desc: King of Britain. Lear is the protagonist whose willingness to believe empty flattery leads to the deaths of many people.

title: King Lear
desc: Lear's eldest daughter who, after professing her deep love for her father, betrays him and plots his murder.

title: King Lear
desc: Lear's second daughter. Regan joins forces with Goneril to destroy their father. Regan initially appears less harsh than her elder sister, but in the end, she proves to be as blood-thirsty as Goneril.

title: King Lear
desc: Lear's youngest daughter. Cordelia genuinely loves her father, but her refusal to flatter him leads to her tragic death.

title: King Lear
desc: Loyal member of the king's court. The Fool assumes the role of protector to Lear when Cordelia is banished.

title: King Lear
desc: Lear's cohort and loyal friend.Gloucester is afoolish old man whose inability to see the truth in his youngest son's words parallels Lear's own difficulties with Goneril and Regan.

title: King Lear
desc: Lear's loyal friend and supporter. Although banished, Kent disguises himself as Caius in an effort to stay close to his king.

title: King Lear
desc: Gloucester's older son. Edgar is Gloucester's only legitimate heir, but he must flee and hide from his father, disguised as Poor Tom, when he comes under suspicion.

title: King Lear
desc: Gloucester's younger, illegitimate, son. He is an opportunist whose ambitions lead him to form a union with Goneril and Regan.

title: King Lear
desc: Goneril's husband. Albany grows in stature during the play and ultimately finds the strength to resist his wife's efforts to have Lear killed.

title: King Lear
desc: Regan's brutal husband. Cornwall is vicious and savage as he tries to eliminate Lear and Gloucester.

title: King Lear
desc: Goneril's steward. Oswald is a willing accomplice to Goneril's plotting and proves a foil to Kent's devotion to Lear.

title: King Lear
desc: Marries Cordelia. France is honorable and willing to support Cordelia's efforts to rescue her father.

title: King Lear
desc: Suitor for Cordelia. Burgundy rejects Cordelia when he discovers that she will bring him no dowry.

title: King Lear
desc: Gloucester's servant.

title: King Lear
desc: Tenant of Gloucester.

title: King Lear
desc: Cornwall's retainers, who attack him in defense of Gloucester.

title: King Lear
desc: Attendant to Cordelia.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: A middle-aged Roman general who rules the Roman Empire along with Lepidus and Octavius Caesar. He is torn between his desire for Cleopatra and the demands of his position as a world ruler.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: The adopted son of Julius Caesar, his grand-uncle, he is only in his early twenties, but he is determined to ultimately be the sole ruler of the Roman Empire.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: As a member of the Triumvirate, he serves as a mediator between Antony and Caesar, the two rivals; he has no real power of his own.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: She is aware of her duties as Queen of Egypt, but she is deeply infatuated with Antony; her heroic courage is revealed when she and Antony are defeated and she chooses to die in Egypt, rather than return to Rome as a captive.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: She becomes engaged to Antony in order to cement a political and military truce between Antony and her brother, Caesar.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: Formerly a Roman, Pompey left Rome with a faction that included several pirates and some of Julius Caesar's navy. He attempted to form his own kingdom, and he is able to cause the Triumvirate trouble by plundering their ships.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: Antony's trusted lieutenant and close friend; eventually, he deserts the man he both admires and pokes fun at, yet he later commits suicide in remorse.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: Another of Antony's officers, he is sent to fight the Parthians. He is a brave and capable general and is absolutely loyal to Antony.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: Another of Antony's officers; he serves as Antony's aide after Enobarbus deserts his general.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: He is one of the first to find Antony after Antony has tried to kill himself. Dercetas deserts to Caesar's faction, taking Antony's sword as a sign that the enemy is almost defeated.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: These friends of Antony's are among those who go with him to Egypt. They long to see their general as he was before he fell in love and forgot about his political and military duties.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: He is a Lieutenant General to Antony, but he deserts Antony's camp for Caesar's faction after Antony's first major defeat.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: He serves as an ambassador from Antony to Caesar.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: She does not appear in the play, but she is Antony's first wife.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: As lieutenant General to Caesar, his strategies help Caesar to win the war against the forces of Antony and Cleopatra.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: An officer and a friend of Caesar; when the triumvirs meet at the house of Lepidus in Rome to effect a truce, Maecenas is present.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: Another friend of Caesar; along with Maecenas, he never judges Antony quite as lightly as does his general.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: This friend of Caesar acts as a messenger and tells Cleopatra on behalf of Caesar that she need not be afraid for her welfare.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: Unlike Proculeius, Dolabella feels pity for Cleopatra and warns her that Caesar's promises to her may be empty ones.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: He also acts as a messenger, telling Cleopatra of Antony's defeat and Octavius's victory.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: She is one of Cleopatra's closest friends and court confidantes.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: Another of Cleopatra's attendants.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: A servant of Cleopatra who acts as a messenger between her and Antony.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: This member of Cleopatra's entourage is a eunuch, a fact which Cleopatra enjoys teasing him about.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: A pirate; he advises Pompey to take the triumvirs captive and have them murdered while they are attending a banquet aboard his ship.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: As a chief officer of Pompey, he helps his general plan strategies.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: He is warlike and ambitious, like Pompey, but he is less unscrupulous than Menas.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: Eros is a friend of Antony's; Gallus is a friend of Caesar's.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: An officer in Ventidius's army.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: Attendants to Cleopatra.

title: Antony and Cleopatra
desc: A fortuneteller who tells Charmian that she will outlive Cleopatra.

title: All's Well That Ends Well
desc: The daughter of a very famous, recently deceased court physician, Helena has the physical and mental attributes which could command the attention of virtually any eligible bachelor, but unfortunately, she does not have the correct social pedigree to entice the man whom she loves, Bertram, a Count's son. Through the use of her native wit and the body of knowledge which she inherits from her father, as well as because of her sheer strength of will, she overcomes all obstacles and wins Bertram. To some commentators on this play, Helena's tactics seem questionable, although no one underestimates her strength of character.

title: All's Well That Ends Well
desc: For several reasons, Bertram seems significantly inferior to Helena. He is under the influence of the patently superficial Parolles, and he lies outright on more than one occasion. Furthermore, he blatantly disregards the king's wishes. To a modern audience, he might seem to have every right to refuse a forced marriage, but to the world which the play inhabits, that is not the case. Besides, Helena is clearly (in everyone else's opinion) a splendid person. The play ends, however, in such an abrupt manner that Shakespeare leaves us wondering just how "well" all has "ended" for Bertram and his "rightful" bride.

title: All's Well That Ends Well
desc: In his prime, the king was a valiant warrior and a staunch friend of Bertram's father. He is utterly charmed by Helena, and he is grateful for the cure which she administers to him. All of this makes his outrage even greater when Bertram refuses to accept Helena as a bride. He exerts his royal authority to force the marriage, and in Shakespeare's scheme of things in the play, he seems to be right in doing so.

title: All's Well That Ends Well
desc: Bertram's mother fully sympathizes with Helena in her state of lovelorn agony, and she goes so far as to say that she will disown her son as a result of his rejection of her adopted "daughter." She does what she can to make things "end well."

title: All's Well That Ends Well
desc: Lafeu is an elderly friend of the Countess and her family. His role is that of adviser and mollifier. He is the first to see through Parolles' schemes, and it is his daughter whose planned marriage to Bertram (before Helena is "resurrected" at the end of the play) will signal a return to good order.

title: All's Well That Ends Well
desc: Lafeu sums up the character of Parolles when he says: "The soul of this man is his clothes." Parolles is the tempter of Bertram as a "prodigal son," and in the end, Parolles is seen as such and rejected.

title: All's Well That Ends Well
desc: The Countess' servant offers comic reflections about several characters in the play, most pointedly about Parolles. His mouth is lewd, and his manner is absurd.

title: All's Well That Ends Well
desc: For a fee, the widow helps Helena arrange and execute the old "bed trick"; here, Bertram is trapped into sleeping with his own wife in the belief that she is another woman.

title: All's Well That Ends Well
desc: Diana is the widow's daughter and Helena's ally in her pursuit of Bertram. She is the bait used to trap Bertram. Diana displays a good deal of wit and a composed bearing under the pressure of the courtly observers during the final "revelation scene."

title: All's Well That Ends Well
desc: A neighbor of the widow.

title: All's Well That Ends Well
role: Two French Lords, the Brothers Dumain
desc: The two noblemen who mastermind the plot to expose Parolles. They are friends to Bertram.
